title: Guidelines for Defining Keyboard Translation Tables
breadcrumbs: Guidelines for Defining Keyboard Translation Tables
text: Guidelines for Defining Keyboard Translation Tables When defining or adapting keyboard translation tables, there are some general guidelines you should follow. Give the table a name the corresponds to the device, for example, DG_AVIION for a Data General AViiON workstation. Do not store your own translation table in the USYS library. This library contains the standard definitions delivered with Uniface. Any keyboard translation tables defined in this library can be overwritten when a new release of Uniface is installed. If you change an existing keyboard translation table, keep in mind that Uniface functions are often defined several times within the same table, for example, for different bit modes or for the GOLD key combinations. Be sure to find all occurrences as you make your changes. When creating your own keyboard translation tables, first map the location of the GOLD key, then use the macro ^GOLD_BASE_SET to define the complete set of keystroke combinations in one mapping. If the GOLD key has not been defined when you use ^GOLD_BASE_SET , the compilation fails. For example, the following mappings create the complete set of GOLD and SUPER key combinations, using the Escape key as GOLD: ^ESC ^GOLD ^CHR ^GOLD_BASE_SET Copy For more information, see Gold and Super Key Combinations . To disable a particular function or key, use ^NUL. ^NUL, and its equivalents ^0 and ^/0 are interpreted as a delimiter. If used in any output sequence, it must be the last character, since anything after the ^NUL is ignored. For example, the F9 key on a VT200 keyboard is not normally used by Uniface. If the keystroke is not defined, the escape sequence generated by the key appears in a field, if the user accidentally presses the key. The following line disables the F9 function key: ^ESC[20~ ^NUL disable F9 To assign both the lowercase and uppercase letter to a sequence, use a caret followed by a lowercase and uppercase letter (for example, ^aA ) as the input sequence. For example, the following line assigns the Uniface function ^STORE to the combination Escape S and Escape s : ^esc^sS ^STORE Copy To assign a string of characters to a key or sequence of keystrokes, use the ^SP code to represent spaces in the text string. Otherwise spaces are interpreted as column separators. For example, the following line assigns a text string to the combination PF1 T: ^escOPT Here^SPis^SPa^SPtext^SPstring that is a text string Copy To assign a text string that is also a Uniface mnemonic, use the ASCII value as the first letter. For example, the following line assigns the string ‘ACCEPT’ to the combination PF1 @: ^escOP@ ^65CCEPT Copy For more information, see Syntax of Meta Characters . To assign more than one mnemonic to a keystroke, separate the mnemonics with a caret. For example, the following line assigns the two functions ^LAST and ^OCCURRENCE to a particular key: ^escOP@ ^LAST^OCCURRENCE Copy Note: This functionality cannot be used with the ^USER_KEY function. A line defining a ^USER_KEY function cannot make additional mappings to other Uniface functions. For more information, see User Key Definitions . Don't assign mnemonics to Ctrl+Alt combinations, because Windows interprets this combination in some language versions as an AltGR key, which generates alphanumeric characters. For example, the following combination of keys will NOT work as shortcut in Uniface: Ctrl+Alt+K . To redefine the subfield separator or a profile character, you should map the new definition to the appropriate position in Font 0. For more information, see Subfield Separator and Profile Characters . In character mode interfaces, you can use the /key command line switch to create keyboard translation table definitions by asking a series of questions. For more information, see /key . Related Links Keyboard Translation Tables A keyboard translation table maps a sequence of input codes received from a keyboard or database to logical function codes and characters recognized by Uniface.
